original_abstract: This article explores the fundamental distinctions between principled and strategic nonviolent movements. While strategic nonviolence has proven to be a highly effective, pragmatic tool for toppling repressive regimes and dictatorships, it frequently falls short when the goal shifts to a deep-seated, progressive transformation of the state. This limitation stems from a core difference in values: principled nonviolence remains deeply skeptical of traditional political power and the coercive nature of the Weberian state. In contrast, strategic movements often seek to seize and utilize the state’s existing machinery—including its monopoly on force—to secure their own political ends. As evidenced by the outcomes in Egypt, Palestine, and Syria, this willingness to compromise often leads to the corruption of the movement's original ideals. This paper argues that only through the radical commitment to pacifism and emancipatory processes central to principled nonviolence can we build social, political, and economic institutions capable of truly transcending Machiavellian politics.
humanized_abstract: DRAFT. PRINCIPLED VS. STRATEGIC NONVIOLENCE The principal difference between these two types of nonviolent movements is rooted in their approach to power and governance. As we explored earlier, strategic nonviolence is a very pragmatic and very effective way of toppiling a dictator or an authoritarian state. However, it is not sufficient for a more profound transformation of the state in the sense of changing the power relations and structures of society in a progressive way. Strategic nonviolent movements typically retain a fairly Machiavellian view of power and the state, the view developed by Max Weber in Politics as a Vocation, which views power as a coercive phenomenon. Consequently, their objective is to take power and to govern society by using the same state apparatus that the dictators had before them and, therefore, by employing the same coercive power. This approach inevitably results in the transformation of any nonviolent movement into a power struggle and in the subsequent corruption of its founding principles. In contrast, a radical pacifist approach to power and governance is essential to creating emancipatory forms of society, politics and economy that transcend the Machiavellian principles that inform state and power relations under authoritarianism and even in democracies.
